title: Direct Fisher Score Estimation for Likelihood Maximization
abstract: We study the problem of likelihood maximization when the likelihood function is intractable but model simulations are readily available. We propose a sequential, gradient-based optimization method that directly models the Fisher score based on a local score matching technique which uses simulations from a localized region around each parameter iterate. By employing a linear parameterization for the surrogate score model, our technique admits a closed-form, least-squares solution. This approach yields a fast, flexible, and efficient approximation to the Fisher score, effectively smoothing the likelihood objective and mitigating the challenges posed by complex likelihood landscapes. We provide theoretical guarantees for our score estimator, including bounds on the bias introduced by the smoothing. Empirical results on a range of synthetic and real-world problems demonstrate the superior performance of our method compared to existing benchmarks.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper presents an approach to estimate maximum likelihood (MLE) estimator is a simulation-based inference setting i.e. where we do not have access to the underlying likelihood distribution but can generate samples from it. The authors approach this by learning Fisher score with a local score matching technique. When the score is parameterized as a linear model, the authors derive a close-form solution for it. The authors present theoretical discussion on convergence and other properties of the estimator, and present various empirical results.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
The main text is well written and easy to follow. The approach is clearly motivated and presented, with discussion on convergence and other properties of the proposed estimator. 

Regarding weakness, see the questions section.

QUESTIONS:
Major questions--

- While linear parameterization for score is convenient in that it gives a closed-form solution, I expect it can be severely constraining in some settings. Have the authors considered using a more flexible model for score and solve Eq. (3) numerically? Some discussion on this should be done in the paper. 
- Along above lines, it also seems that currently the authors fit a new score model (W) at every point, but can this be amortized? Similarly, will have a training dataset (as is generally done in SBI) help with more flexible score model? Again, some discussion on this will improve the paper.
- The authors discuss the impact of $\sigma_t$, but is there any guidance on how to choose it for a new problem? Along the same lines, do the authors consider varying/adapting $\sigma$ over iterations (e.g. depending on where they are in parameter space or as they get closer to MLE).
- How does the approach scale with dimensions, both parameter and data dimensions? If I understand correctly, the highest dimensions in current examples is 20. Again, some guidance along these lines is necessary for new applications. 

Minor points -- 
- I would recommend the authors include the algorithm-box in the main-text in the updated version (given the extra page). 
- Dimensionality of the parameters is not given in MNIST example
- Convergence of FSM is difficult to see in Fig. 3, right panel.

LIMITATIONS:
While the paper does discuss some limitations, I believe it can be expanded.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper tackles the problem of maximizing a log-likelihood $\log p(x|\theta)$, with data $x$ and parameters $\theta$, in the simulation-based inference setting, i.e., when $p(x|\theta)$ is intractable but model simulations can be obtained. The authors present an adaptation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) algorithm, where the gradient $\nabla_\theta \log p(x|\theta)$ is approximated via score matching at each iteration. To be more precise, given the current parameter value $\theta_t$, they consider a Gaussian prior $p(\theta|\theta_t)$ centered in $\theta_t$ (with arbitrary scalar variance $\sigma$ defined as a hyperparameter) and propose to approximate $\nabla_{\theta=\theta_t} \log p(x|\theta)$ by the conditional expectation of $\nabla_\theta \log p(x|\theta)$ over the posterior distribution $p(\theta|x, \theta_t)\propto p(x|\theta)p(\theta|\theta_t)$ (which I denote here as the "Bayes estimator"). Interestingly, the L2 loss induced by this conditional expectation can be written into a simulation-free loss similar to Implicit Score Matching (ISM), coined Local Fisher Score Matching, but with the advantage of bypassing the need of computing divergence of the approximation function. In practice, they minimize this loss over the class of linear functions wrt x, which results in an explicit minimizer and avoids intensive use of computational budget at each gradient step.

On the theoretical side, they show that the Bayes estimator of $\nabla_\theta \log p(x|\theta)$ can be easily interpreted as the score of a smoothed version of the original likelihood (namely, Gaussian smoothing which is widely used in zeroth-order optimization), and quantify the bias of this estimator with interpretable quantities. Interestingly, they demonstrate that the quality empirical estimator (i.e., the linear approximation of the Bayes estimator) is intimately induced by a trade-off on the scalar variance $\sigma$. Finally, they present numerical experiments where they compare their method with a KDE-based SGD approach, and evaluate the robustness of their algorithm with respect to the hyperparameter $\sigma$ and the dimension of the problem for various tasks where ground truth can be made available (cosmology, generator inversion..).

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths**
- I find that the paper is very well written, I enjoyed very much the reading : the organization of the sections and the results is well chosen and easy to follow
- although the presented approach may be simple (combining Gaussian prior with linear parameterization), the resulting algorithm is well justified in theory and very efficient in practice (notably due to the lack of divergence computation in the ISM-like objective) : the overall idea is very nice and has been thought to handle reasonable computational budget, which is key for practical applications
- this paper present solid theoretical results (Theorems 5.1, 5.2, 5.3), which are interpretable and may help for applications
- the proposed algorithm demonstrates better robustness to increasing dimension than previous work

**Weaknesses** 
- in my opinion, the major weakness of this algorithm is the choice of the scalar parameter $\sigma$ for practical applications. As explained above, it plays the central role in the quality of the Bayes estimator (a low $\sigma$ would increase the variance, while a large $\sigma$ would increase the bias) and has to be carefully tuned. As far as I understand, this hyperparameter has been tuned in the presented numerical experiments based on access to ground truth (hence, by minimizing the prediction error wrt the optimal parameter). However, in practice, such tuning would seem impossible since both ground truth and likelihood function are not available... Furthermore, this hyperparameter is fixed from the beginning of the optimization procedure, which may not be optimal in practice.
- a second weakness (which is acknowledged by the authors) lies in the linear parameterization of the approximated gradient : the underlying assumption is that $x$ is distributed according to a Gaussian distribution given $\theta$, i.e., $p(x|\theta)\approx N(x; f(\theta), g(\theta))$, which may be too simple for real-world applications.

QUESTIONS:
- In practice (i.e., without access to ground truth), what is the strategy to tune $\sigma$ ?
- Have you considered (i) taking a diagonal covariance matrix to model the Gaussian prior $p(\theta|\theta_t)$ and (ii) optimizing the covariance on-the-fly (i.e., making it depend on $\theta_t$) ?
- Have you considered a neural network parameterization of the Bayes estimator combined with a joint SGD procedure (i.e, taking some local SGD steps wrt the FSM loss (2) at each global gradient step) ? I have the feeling that it won't be too much costly, while dramatically increasing the expressivity of the model.

LIMITATIONS:
The authors acknowledge two limitations of their work : (i) the potential lack of expressivity from the linear parameterization and (ii) the careful tuning of the hyperparameter $\sigma$.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper introduces a novel method for likelihood-free MLE by estimating the Fisher score via local score matching around each parameter iterate. A linear surrogate model yields a closed-form estimator. The method is connected to Gaussian smoothing and outperforms alternative approaches in simulation efficiency.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths**

- Novel, theoretically grounded estimator for likelihood-free Fisher score.
- Closed-form linear model enables fast and stable optimization.
- Strong empirical results across diverse SBI tasks.
- Clear connection to Gaussian smoothing.

**Weaknesses**
- $\theta_t$ and $\theta_j$ notation may confuse readers due to overlapping roles.
- Limited to linear score models; unclear if more expressive models would help.
- Not amortized; optimization must be redone per dataset.

QUESTIONS:
- Have you tried more flexible (e.g., neural) score models?
- How sensitive is the method to the choice of $\sigma^2$ in high dimensions?
- Your estimator is based on a local posterior $p(\theta \mid x, \theta_t) \propto p(x \mid \theta) q(\theta \mid \theta_t)$ induced by the proposal — this resembles an empirical Bayes procedure where the prior adapts during optimization. Could you comment on this connection, and whether an EB perspective might offer additional insights or extensions?

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors present a new likelyhood function gradient estimator by approximating the fisher score with a local linear approximation formed by local sampling of a parameterized likelyhood with a known distribution. This method is a variation of the previously discovered score matching technique, can apply to settings where users have access to samples from a model, but cannot write down a closed form for the model. THey connect this work to gaussian smoothing, as the sampled score approximates a smoothed version of the original score gradient. They prove results charaterizing bias and convergence of this score approximator, and evaluate it on toy data, gravitational lensing data, and generator inversion.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- Clever mathematics to compute their score function gradient 
- Good applications to scientific domains like lensing
- A nice theoretical contribution
- Good supporting theorems like bias and convergence analysis
- A simple idea that is reasonably quite general and of use to the community

Weaknesses:
- Hard to undersntad the magnitufe of improvement in fig 4. It seems pretty marginal from a quick glance. 
- Demonstrations of sample efficientcy seem to be on simulated data, a real data sample efficiency analysis would be more convincing
- A little dense, a more direct introduction might be a big benefit for the general ML reader. Likewise an overview figure to help readers quickly grok the main idea

QUESTIONS:
- You use a local linear model, what changes if this local model is nonlinear? 
- Does this work when the parameter spaces are complex and the gaussian neighborhood sampling assumption doesent seem reasonable. How do the conclusions of the work change in this case.
 - How does performance scale with dimensionality of the parameter space

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (spotlight)

COMMENT:
The authors introduce a general method for maximizing likelihood when it is intractable to compute, in the simulation-based inference setting (when samples from any p_theta are obtainable), based on Fisher score matching (with linear parameterization). The paper is supported with both theory and experiments. After discussion, all authors voted for acceptance. The main strength is that the paper gives a new, very generally applicable method for a generally important problem and demonstrates its effectiveness. The discussion centered around answering reviewer requests for experiments (in the high-dimension setting and in comparison to neural network models) and hyperparameter tuning, both of which reviewers found were answered satisfactorily.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
